---
title: "Your Paper Title"
author: |
  Author One\textsuperscript{\normalsize\orcidlink{0000-0000-0000-0001}}\thanks{Email: \href{mailto:author.one@example.org}{author.one@example.org}. Address: Department, University, City, Country.} ,
  Author Two\textsuperscript{\normalsize\orcidlink{0000-0000-0000-0002}}\thanks{Email: \href{mailto:author.two@example.org}{author.two@example.org}. Address: Department, University, City, Country.} , and
  Author Three\textsuperscript{\normalsize\orcidlink{0000-0000-0000-0003}}\thanks{Email: \href{mailto:author.three@example.org}{author.three@example.org}. Corresponding author. Address: Department, University, City, Country.}
date: today
date-format: "[This revision:] D MMMM YYYY"
abstract: |
  This is a self-documenting Quarto paper template. The body of the
  paper explains how to use the qkit paper format: YAML metadata,
  cross-references, equations, theorem environments, citations,
  callouts, code chunks, and the in-paper Appendix. Replace this
  abstract with your own (150--250 words covering question, data,
  method, and main findings).

  \bigskip
  \noindent\textbf{Keywords:} keyword one, keyword two, keyword three

  \noindent\textbf{JEL Classification:} A00, B00, C00
keywords:
  - keyword one
  - keyword two
  - keyword three
thanks: |
  We thank seminar and conference participants for helpful comments.
  Replace this acknowledgments block with your own.
bibliography: references.bib

format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    documentclass: article
    classoption:
      - "12pt"
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    linestretch: 2
    indent: true
    fontsize: 12pt
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: red
    citecolor: blue
    urlcolor: magenta
    number-sections: true
    keep-tex: true
    subparagraph: true
    include-in-header: preamble.tex
---

```{=tex}
\newpage
```

# Introduction {#sec-intro}

This template is a worked example. Each section below demonstrates a
Quarto feature you can use when writing your own paper; replace the
demonstration text with your own prose as you fill in the template.

**Markdown basics.** Use single asterisks for *italic*, doubles for
**bold**, triples for ***bold italic***, and backticks for
`inline code`. Hyperlinks are inline: see the
[Quarto documentation](https://quarto.org) for the full reference.

**Cross-references.** Prefix any section heading's label with `sec-`
(for example `# Introduction {#sec-intro}`) and reference it inline
with `@sec-intro`. Equations use the `eq-` prefix, tables `tbl-`,
figures `fig-`. The remainder of the paper is organized as follows.
Section @sec-theory shows how to write theorems and labeled
equations. Section @sec-data shows how to insert tables. Section
@sec-method demonstrates citations and equation references. Section
@sec-results covers Quarto callouts. Sections @sec-additional and
@sec-robust round out the body. Section @sec-conc closes.
Appendices @sec-app-vardesc and @sec-app-derivations demonstrate
`\appendix`-style numbering.

**Citations.** Cite a `.bib` entry with `[@example2025]` for a
parenthetical (Doe and Roe 2025) or with `@example2025` for an
in-text citation; the qkit paper template uses Pandoc's `citeproc`
with Chicago author-date style by default. The bibliography renders
at the `# References` anchor placed after the Conclusion.

# Theoretical Framework {#sec-theory}

A labeled display equation uses the syntax
`$$...$$ {#eq-label}`. For example,

$$
y_{it} = \alpha_i + \delta_t + \beta\, x_{it} + \gamma'\mathbf{Z}_{it} + \varepsilon_{it}
$$ {#eq-baseline}

is referenced inline with `@eq-baseline`. The qkit preamble forces
symmetric vertical space above and below every display equation, so
the gap looks the same whether the line preceding the equation is
short or long. See @eq-baseline above for the baseline specification.

Five theorem-style environments are predefined via `amsthm`:
`theorem`, `proposition`, `lemma`, `definition`, and `assumption`.
Use any of them with Pandoc's fenced-div syntax. For example:

::: {.assumption}
The error term $\varepsilon_{it}$ is mean-zero and conditionally
independent of the focal regressor $x_{it}$ given the unit fixed
effect $\alpha_i$.
:::

::: {.proposition}
Under the assumption above, the within-unit OLS estimator of $\beta$
in @eq-baseline is consistent.
:::

## Testable Predictions {#sec-theory-predictions}

Subsection headings render italic, not bold --- a deliberate qkit
style choice that gives section headings the visual weight and
subsection headings the contrast of italic. Section numbering carries
a period after the digit (`1. Introduction`, `1.1 Subsection`), also
a preamble tweak.

# Data {#sec-data}

Variable definitions are deferred to Appendix @sec-app-vardesc. This
section shows how to insert a table via a `knitr` chunk and how to
reference it from the prose.

## Data Sources and Sample Construction {#sec-data-sources}

Markdown bullet lists work as expected:

- One bullet per line, hyphen + space at the start.
- Sub-bullets indent four spaces.
    - Like this nested entry.
- Empty lines between items create a "loose" list with extra space
  between entries.

## Variable Definitions {#sec-data-vars}

Numbered lists use the same syntax:

1. Use `1.`, `2.`, `3.` at the start of each item.
2. Numbering is automatic; Quarto renumbers on render.
3. To restart numbering, leave a blank line and start a new list.

## Summary Statistics {#sec-data-summary}

Reference Table @tbl-summary for descriptive statistics. The
following `knitr` chunk renders a table when given real data and
`eval: true`; it's currently a placeholder.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-summary
#| tbl-cap: "Summary statistics for the example panel."
#| echo: false
#| eval: false
# knitr::kable(your_data_frame, booktabs = TRUE)
```

Chunk options live in the `#| key: value` block at the top of the
chunk. The `label:` value drives cross-references (use `tbl-` for
tables, `fig-` for figures), `tbl-cap:` sets the caption, and
`eval:` controls whether the code actually runs.

# Methodology {#sec-method}

To cite a reference, write `@key` for in-text or `[@key]` for
parenthetical. The bibliography reads from the `bibliography:`
YAML field --- by default `references.bib`. For example, the model
in @eq-baseline mirrors the specification studied by @example2025.

A second labeled equation lets us reference the estimator:

$$
\widehat{\beta} = \arg\min_{\beta}\, \sum_{i,t} \bigl(y_{it} - X_{it}'\beta\bigr)^{2}.
$$ {#eq-ols}

Equations @eq-baseline and @eq-ols can be referenced together in a
single sentence, and Quarto formats the cross-reference list
automatically.

# Main Results {#sec-results}

This section demonstrates Quarto callouts. Five variants are
available; each renders as a tinted box with a coloured left rule.

::: {.callout-note}
A `note` callout is for general asides --- context the reader might
want but doesn't need to follow the main argument.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
A `tip` callout flags a practical recommendation or shortcut.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
A `warning` callout highlights a caveat or limitation.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
An `important` callout draws attention to something the reader
must not miss.
:::

::: {.callout-caution}
A `caution` callout is the strongest emphasis variant; use it
sparingly.
:::

## Baseline Estimates {#sec-results-baseline}

Estimating @eq-baseline on the full sample yields a point estimate
of $\widehat{\beta} = 0.12$ (standard error 0.03). Quarto knows
that `@eq-baseline` should render as a parenthetical equation
reference because the label was declared with the `eq-` prefix.

## Heterogeneity {#sec-results-hetero}

Subgroup estimates range from 0.04 to 0.21. Inline math uses single
dollar signs: $\widehat{\beta}_{\text{low}} = 0.04$ and
$\widehat{\beta}_{\text{high}} = 0.21$.

## Mechanisms {#sec-results-mechanism}

Two-thirds of the headline effect operates contemporaneously; the
remainder unfolds with a one-period lag.

# Additional Analyses {#sec-additional}

Block quotations are rendered with 1.5cm indentation on both sides
--- a qkit preamble tweak that matches the look of standard
working-paper templates:

> Quoted material goes here. The custom `quote` environment is
> indented an extra 1.5cm on each side relative to the body text,
> matching a common econ/finance convention. Multiple lines stay
> within the block as long as each starts with `>`.

Footnotes use the inline syntax `^[...]`.^[Like this --- a footnote
attached to the word "this". Footnotes render at the bottom of the
page they appear on.]

# Robustness {#sec-robust}

Code spans use single backticks (`` `code` ``); fenced code blocks
use triple backticks with an optional language tag for syntax
highlighting:

```latex
\textit{italic text} and \textbf{bold text}.
```

Inline math: $\widehat{\beta} \xrightarrow{p} \beta$ as
$n \to \infty$. Display math: see @eq-baseline.

# Conclusion {#sec-conc}

A paper template that documents itself: each section above
demonstrates a different Quarto feature. When you replace the
demonstration prose with your own analysis, the headings,
equations, theorem environments, citations, callouts, and quotes
will pick up the qkit styling automatically.

```{=tex}
\newpage
```

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::

```{=tex}
\newpage
\appendix
\setcounter{table}{0}
\setcounter{figure}{0}
\renewcommand{\thesection}{Appendix \Alph{section}}
\renewcommand{\thetable}{\Alph{section}.\arabic{table}}
\renewcommand{\thefigure}{\Alph{section}.\arabic{figure}}
\renewcommand{\theequation}{\Alph{section}.\arabic{equation}}
```

# Variable Definitions {#sec-app-vardesc}

The in-paper Appendix demonstrates appendix-specific numbering. The
`\appendix` macro --- called from a raw-LaTeX block just before this
heading --- resets the section counter and remaps `\thesection` so
headings render as "Appendix A.", "Appendix B.", and so on. Tables,
figures, and equations within an appendix section pick up the
appendix letter as a prefix (A.1, A.2, B.1, ...).

In a real paper, this section would carry a `tabularx` or
`booktabs` table listing every variable used in @sec-data, with
its definition, units, and source.

# Auxiliary Derivations {#sec-app-derivations}

A display equation inside Appendix B renders as Equation B.1:

$$
\widehat{\beta} = (X'X)^{-1} X' y.
$$ {#eq-app-baseline}

Compare to @eq-baseline in the main text --- that one is numbered
plainly (e.g. 1, 2, ...) while @eq-app-baseline carries the
B-prefix.

Proofs use a `.proof` div, which `amsthm` renders with an italic
"Proof." header and a closing $\square$:

::: {.proof}
*Sketch.* Within-unit demeaning eliminates the fixed effect; the
demeaned regressor satisfies the orthogonality condition stated in
the assumption; OLS on the demeaned variables is therefore
consistent. $\square$
:::
